sections.title: Choosing appropriate units (m vs cm)
sections.prose_summary: The chapter opens by activating prior knowledge of length units — metre and centimetre — through a matching activity. Students identify which unit is appropriate for everyday quantities such as the height of India Gate, the length of a handkerchief, or the depth of a well. This is a concrete-to-symbolic warm-up that anchors unit sense before new units are introduced.

tasks.description: Identify the appropriate units (m or cm) for: height of India Gate, handkerchief, well, mobile phone, elephant's trunk, shirt buttons.

sections.title: Different units but same measure
sections.prose_summary: Shikha and Sonu's sari-measuring context introduces the equivalence 100 cm = 1 m through a double number line, where students match measurements in cm to their metre equivalents. The double number line makes the multiplicative relationship (×100) visible and spatial, sitting at the pictorial level of the concrete→pictorial→symbolic path.

tasks.book_ref: Different Units but Same Measure, p.57
tasks.description: Find which measures (given in cm and m) represent the same sari or stole using the double number line.

sections.title: Measuring long distances — kilometre
sections.prose_summary: The chapter introduces the kilometre (1,000 m = 1 km) as the unit for long distances, and presents real measuring tools — long tape, rodometer, rangefinder, laser distance meter. Students physically measure 100 m and 200 m on the playground, develop a personal step-distance relationship, and apply it to estimate distances around them. A table of rope lengths asks students to compute how many ropes of a given length make 1 km, building multiplicative understanding of the km definition.

tasks.description: Measure 100 m and 200 m on playground with long tape; walk lines and count steps; use step-distance relationship to find distances of at least 3 locations; identify nearest and farthest from home; write distances in increasing order; name a location ≥1,000 m away.
Complete the table: for each rope length (100 m, 10 m, 200 m, 500 m, 250 m), find how many ropes are needed to make 1 km.

sections.title: Kilometre race — applying km in context
sections.prose_summary: The Kilometre Race context embeds km-and-m arithmetic in race-planning logistics: positioning water stations, children, and flags at regular intervals along a 3 km course. Students solve three related problems that require partitioning km distances into equal parts and counting placements, moving firmly to the symbolic level.

tasks.description: Water stations every 500 m in a 3-km race — how many stations? At what positions from the start?
Children at 300 m intervals to direct runners in 3-km race — how many children needed? At what positions from the start?
Red and blue flags alternately every 50 m in a 3-km race — how many red and blue flags are needed till the finish line?

sections.title: Longest train journey — km arithmetic with data
sections.prose_summary: The Vivek Express route from Dibrugarh to Kanniyakumari provides a real data table of station distances in km. Students read the table to compute totals, differences between stations, and make comparisons — practicing km subtraction and reasoning about relative distances in a rich geographical context.

tasks.description: Find the total length of the route from Dibrugarh to Kanniyakumari.
Find the distance between Vijayawada and Jalpaiguri Road.
Find the distance between Vijayawada and Visakhapatnam.
Which two stations are farther apart — Guwahati and Dimapur, or Bhubaneswar and Jalpaiguri Road?
Find the distance between Guwahati and Coimbatore JN.

sections.title: The world of small things — millimetre
sections.prose_summary: This section zooms in to introduce the millimetre (mm) as the unit for small objects — sprouts, screws, nails. Students examine a ruler, count the ten small marks between two centimetre marks, and discover 1 cm = 10 mm. Two hands-on tasks follow: measuring lines in a geometric design to the nearest mm, and a four-day seed-sprouting experiment where daily sprout growth is recorded in mm — placing measurement in a living, observational context.

tasks.description: Examine the scale markings between 1 cm and 2 cm; count mm marks; discover 1 cm = 10 mm.
Measure the lines in the geometric design and write their measurements in cm and mm.
Soak moong/chana seeds; over 4 days take one seed each day and measure sprout length in mm; record in table.

sections.title: Relationships between different units
sections.prose_summary: The summary relationship box (10 mm = 1 cm; 100 cm = 1 m; 1,000 m = 1 km) anchors the full unit hierarchy. Students first draw lines to exact mm lengths — bridging pictorial and symbolic — then use three sets of double number lines (mm↔cm, cm↔m, m↔km) to fill in scaling relationships, and finally complete a large conversion drill (14 sub-parts) converting across all four units in both directions.

tasks.description: Draw lines of lengths 5 cm 5 mm, 3 cm 6 mm, 8 cm 3 mm, 36 mm, 67 mm in notebook using a scale.
Fill in the blanks in the cm↔mm double number line (1 cm/10 mm to 50 cm/__ mm).
Fill in the blanks in the m↔cm double number line (1 m/__ cm to 21 m/__ cm).
Fill in the blanks in the km↔m double number line (1 km/1,000 m to 82 km/__ m).

Convert: 4 cm 5 mm to mm; 89 mm to cm mm; 234 cm to mm; 514 mm to cm mm; 6 m 34 cm to cm; 20 m 12 cm to cm; 397 m to cm; 5,792 cm to m cm; 9,108 cm to m cm; 34 km to m; 6,870 m to km m; 10,552 m to km m; 29 km 30 m to m; 32 km 359 m to m.

sections.title: Adding and subtracting lengths
sections.prose_summary: Worked examples model two strategies for adding and subtracting compound lengths (km+m; m+cm): adding like units separately then regrouping, and converting everything to one unit before operating. Both column layouts are shown side by side. Students then solve real-world problems involving ribbon addition, distance subtraction, cable car totals, and cross-unit comparison with difference-finding.

tasks.description: Rani has ribbon rolls of 3 m 75 cm and 2 m 25 cm. How much ribbon in total?
Bhopal to Sanchi is 48 km 700 m; Bhadbhada Ghat waterfall is 17 km 900 m from Bhopal. How far is Sanchi from the waterfall?
Gulmarg Gondola section 1 is 2 km 300 m and section 2 is 2 km 650 m. Find total distance covered.
Circle the bigger length and find the difference for 7 pairs: 11 mm vs 1 cm; 26 mm vs 2 cm; 20 cm vs 201 mm; 1,020 mm vs 1 m; 2 m vs 245 cm; 5,678 m vs 6 km; 6 km 1,480 m vs 7 km 479 m.

sections.title: Multiplying and dividing lengths
sections.prose_summary: A shirt-cloth problem models multiplication of a compound length (1 m 80 cm × 20) by breaking the quantity into like units and multiplying each part separately. A division problem (road-laying over 6 days) applies the inverse. A cloth-cost double number line develops proportional reasoning between length and price, and an embroidery thread problem requires multi-step multiplication then a cost calculation.

tasks.description: Shop sells cloth at ₹100 for 5 m. Use the double number line to find cost or length for various quantities up to ₹2,000.
Anita needs 1 m thread per 50 cm sari border. How much thread for 5 m sari? Thread costs ₹50 per metre — how much money needed?
Road 12 km 600 m long is completed in 6 equal days. How much road is laid each day?

sections.title: Other units — feet, inches, estimation
sections.prose_summary: The chapter closes by connecting the metric system to informal units used in everyday Indian life — feet and inches. Students examine a ruler to find inches marked alongside centimetres and discover 1 inch = 2 cm 5 mm and 1 foot = 30 cm. Estimation tasks ask students to use known references (a single-storeyed house, an electric pole) to judge the height of a building, a tree, and the depth of a well. A final Try This puzzle tasks students to complete a 4×4 grid of operation signs — an engaging reasoning challenge.

tasks.description: Estimate the height of the tallest building in your neighbourhood; height of the tallest tree; depth of a well or swimming pool — share reasoning and reference used.
Look at ruler to find inches; find how many cm and mm equal 1 inch; note 1 inch = 2 cm 5 mm, 1 foot = 12 inches or 30 cm.
Stand against wall, mark your height; measure distance floor-to-mark in feet and inches; find tallest student in class and their height in feet and inches.
Put appropriate operation signs (+, –, ×, ÷) in a 4×4 grid such that each row, column, and 4×4 box does not repeat any sign.
